museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: The most familiar Space Invaders release framing lands here: June 1978 Japan, the moment the arcade year starts to feel different.
museum.curatorNote: June uses the common Japan-release framing while still acknowledging April publication and later regional rollout.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched moments from an early videogame year, with broad context separated from confident month-level claims.

items.title: Space Invaders is released in Japan in many histories
items.body: The June framing is the one most often used for the public arcade release and the beginning of the craze.
items.sourceNote: Many Space Invaders histories cite June 1978 Japan release.

items.title: One cabinet changes arcade economics
items.body: Space Invaders will become a reason for operators to rethink what a videogame cabinet can earn.

items.title: The screen becomes a defensive line
items.body: Unlike Pong descendants, this is not sport or abstraction: it feels like a small war at the bottom of a monitor.

items.title: Britain receives the shockwave through arcades
items.body: The UK impact is public first: cafes, arcades, amusement halls and queues.

items.title: The home console now has a problem
items.body: Living-room machines cannot yet reproduce the arcade event that people are starting to talk about.
items.sourceNote: Home Space Invaders arrives later on Atari VCS in 1980.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: A deliberately sparse shelf: only releases supported by the sources reviewed are displayed.

items.title: Space Invaders
items.body: Taito's public arcade phenomenon, listed in many histories as a June Japan release.
items.certainty: widely cited month; April publication caveat
items.sourceNote: Many histories cite June 1978; other sources cite April 19 publication.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Four objects explaining the month: public cabinets, home consoles, microcomputers and control technology.

items.title: Space Invaders arcade board
items.body: By June 1978, Taito's cabinet represents the new centre of arcade gravity, even where local rollout is still uneven.
items.sourceNote: Space Invaders hardware and release histories.

items.title: Atari VCS before its killer app
items.body: The cartridge console exists, but it has not yet received the arcade conversion that will transform its sales.
items.sourceNote: Atari VCS and Space Invaders VCS context.

items.title: Trackball control
items.body: Atari Football helps make the trackball a memorable public control surface.

items.title: Microcomputer magazine culture
items.body: Home computer games still travel through listings, ads and specialist computing magazines more than through ordinary game retail.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: Paper remains the map: computing, electronics and arcade context before games media fully settles.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Networked play exists at the edges, but ordinary players live in a world of paper, cabinets and local knowledge.

items.title: Online life was not the ordinary visitor experience
items.body: For most players in June 1978, games are discovered through arcades, shops, magazines, clubs and word of mouth rather than online services.
items.sourceNote: Included to avoid projecting later online habits backward.

items.title: Institutional networks remain the edge case
items.body: PLATO, university systems and early networked computing matter historically, but they are not normal UK consumer gaming.
items.sourceNote: PLATO and early networked game context.

items.title: Paper is still the search engine
items.body: Magazines, adverts, catalogues, manuals and club newsletters explain what machines and games exist.
items.sourceNote: BYTE, Creative Computing, PCW and electronics-magazine context.

items.title: The arcade is the live feed
items.body: High scores, queues and overheard advice make the cabinet a public information system.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A young medium, a patchy record, and a future split between public machines and home hardware.

items.title: The future was still public
items.body: The arcade suddenly feels invaded: one cabinet can pull a room into a shared rhythm of shots, shields and descending pressure.

items.title: The home shelf was changing shape
items.body: Home games still feel smaller than the public machine. The VCS is promising, but the arcade owns the spectacle.

items.title: Britain saw the edges first
items.body: In Britain, this is a public memory first: cafes, arcades, seaside amusements, coins and the sound of aliens advancing.

items.title: The record is still uneven
items.body: Exact dates are often regional, trade-led or retrospective. The exhibit keeps month-level certainty separate from year-level context.

editorNotes: Initial AI-generated research draft for 1978. Sparse months intentionally avoid invented releases.
Magazine cover images are reconstructed placeholders and should be replaced with verified/licensed scans if used editorially.
Several arcade, console and computer-game entries are year-level or source-conflicted; sourceNote fields mark uncertainty.
